# NHS Improvement, A Just Culture Guide (deep reference)

**Source:** NHS Improvement (2018). *A Just Culture Guide — Supporting consistent, constructive and fair evaluation of the actions of staff involved in patient safety incidents*. A3 landscape poster, single page. Based on the work of Professor James Reason and the National Patient Safety Agency's Incident Decision Tree. Licence: **Open Government Licence v3.0** (UK public-sector open licence; CC BY 4.0-equivalent for redistribution and derivative purposes). Canonical URL: https://cpcw.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/19/2018/03/180316-NHSi_just_culture_guide_A3.pdf (mirror; publisher page at improvement.nhs.uk has been retired following the 2022 merger of NHS Improvement into NHS England).
**Scope:** open
**Structure:** A single A3 landscape poster comprising: title and subtitle; a four-bullet "Please note" caveat block; a three-paragraph preamble framing purpose and use; five sequential tests (Q1 deliberate harm; Q2 health, with three sub-questions; Q3 foresight, with three sub-questions; Q4 substitution, with three sub-questions; Q5 mitigating circumstances); and seven recommendation end-states distributed across the branches of the tree. The decision tree is the load-bearing apparatus; the preamble carries the principle that "action singling out an individual is rarely appropriate" and frames how to use the guide; the recommendations carry the operational guidance for each branch.
**Citation style:** `(Section, "Subsection")` — for example `(Q3, "Foresight test")` for the foresight test, `(Preamble, "Please note")` for the four caveats, `(Q1, "Recommendation A")` for the deliberate-harm response. The source is a single page with no page-number anchors; sections are named by the question number (Q1-Q5) and the recommendation labels (A-G) introduced in the converted markdown to make the seven distinct end-states individually citable. The original poster does not letter its recommendations; the A-G labels are a citation convenience and are noted as such.
**Coverage:** complete. The single-page poster has been read in full; all four "Please note" caveats, all three preamble paragraphs, all five tests with all sub-questions, and all seven recommendation end-states are represented in the deep reference. No section has been omitted.

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

The guide's central thesis is that the appropriate response to a member of staff involved in a patient safety incident should differ according to the *circumstances in which the error was made* rather than the *outcome that occurred* (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The poster states that "This guide supports a conversation between managers about whether a staff member involved in a patient safety incident requires specific individual support or intervention to work safely" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use"), and operationalises this through a five-test decision tree. The tests are sequential, with each test gating the next; only one action (or failure to act) is taken through the tree at a time (Preamble, "Please note", bullet 4).

A second load-bearing claim is structural: "action singling out an individual is rarely appropriate — most patient safety issues have deeper causes and require wider action" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use") [AR]. The decision tree is calibrated to this prior: the foresight test (Q3) and the substitution test (Q4) — together accounting for four of the seven end-states — both route default to *not appropriate to single out* the individual, with the patient safety incident investigation pointing to wider actions (Q3, "Recommendation D"; Q4, "Recommendation E"). Only the deliberate-harm branch (Q1) and the no-mitigating-circumstances branch of Q5 route to management action directed at the individual, and the no-mitigation branch still pairs that action with a directive that "the patient safety incident investigation should indicate the wider actions needed to improve safety for future patients" (Q5, "Recommendation G").

A third load-bearing claim is that the guide is a *conversation aid, not a substitute* for either investigation or HR advice (Preamble, "Please note"). Four explicit caveats in the "Please note" block delineate the guide's scope: (i) it is not a replacement for an investigation; only a full investigation can identify the underlying causes; (ii) it can be used at any point of an investigation, but may need to be revisited as more information becomes available; (iii) it does not replace HR advice and should be used in conjunction with organisational policy; (iv) only one action (or failure to act) is taken through the guide at a time, and multiple actions in one incident must be considered separately [V] (Preamble, "Please note").

A fourth load-bearing claim is that *making the approach explainable* is itself part of a just culture: "An important part of a just culture is being able to explain the approach that will be taken if an incident occurs" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The guide is positioned for use by *all parties* — managers, staff, patients, families — as a communication tool, "as a reference point for organisational HR and incident reporting policies, and as a communication tool to help staff, patients and families understand how the appropriate response to a member of staff involved in an incident can and should differ according to the circumstances in which an error was made" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The end the guide serves is dual: "protecting staff from unfair targeting" and protecting patients "by removing the tendency to treat wider patient safety issues as individual issues" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use").

## Part I: Caveats and framing (Preamble)

### Four caveats on use ("Please note")

The poster opens with four caveats that set the guide's scope (Preamble, "Please note"):

1. **Not a replacement for investigation.** The guide is not a replacement for an investigation of a patient safety incident; only a full investigation can identify the underlying causes that need to be acted on to reduce the risk of future incidents [V] (Preamble, "Please note", bullet 1).
2. **Re-entrant.** The guide "can be used at any point of an investigation, but the guide may need to be revisited as more information becomes available" [V] (Preamble, "Please note", bullet 2). The guide is therefore not a once-only assessment; the inputs to the tests are facts surfaced by the investigation, and as those facts change, the path through the tree may change [AP].
3. **Not a replacement for HR advice.** The guide does not replace HR advice and should be used in conjunction with organisational policy [V] (Preamble, "Please note", bullet 3).
4. **One action at a time.** The guide can only be used to take one action (or failure to act) through the guide at a time. If multiple actions are involved in an incident they must be considered separately [V] (Preamble, "Please note", bullet 4) [AR]. This caveat enforces a discipline of decomposition: an incident comprising several distinct actions or omissions is not a single trip through the tree but multiple trips, one per action.

### Purpose: a conversation between managers

The guide supports a conversation between managers about whether a staff member involved in a patient safety incident requires specific individual support or intervention to work safely [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use") [AR]. The prior is explicit: "Action singling out an individual is rarely appropriate — most patient safety issues have deeper causes and require wider action" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The actions of staff involved in an incident should **not** automatically be examined using the guide; the guide is useful when "the investigation of an incident begins to suggest a concern about an individual action" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use") [AR]. The guide is described as highlighting "important principles that need to be considered before formal management action is directed at an individual staff member" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use").

### Use as communication tool

The guide is positioned as both an internal decision aid and an external communication tool (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). It serves as "a reference point for organisational HR and incident reporting policies, and as a communication tool to help staff, patients and families understand how the appropriate response to a member of staff involved in an incident can and should differ according to the circumstances in which an error was made" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The dual purpose is stated: "protecting staff from unfair targeting" and protecting patients "by removing the tendency to treat wider patient safety issues as individual issues" [V] (Preamble, "Purpose and use") [AR].

## Part II: The five sequential tests

The decision tree applies five tests in order: deliberate harm, health, foresight, substitution, mitigating circumstances. Each test is gated on the prior: a positive finding at any test routes to a corresponding recommendation that exits the tree; a negative finding proceeds to the next test. The structure expresses the source's argument that culpability is the exception, not the default — the tree is built to surface system, training, supervision, or mitigating factors before management action is directed at the individual.

### Q1. Deliberate harm test

The first test asks a single question (Q1, "Deliberate harm test"):

**1a.** "Was there any intention to cause harm?" [V] (Q1, "Deliberate harm test").

A Yes answer routes to Recommendation A (deliberate-harm response, see Part III below); a No answer routes to Q2. The deliberate-harm test is the only test in the tree that addresses *intent*; all subsequent tests address conditions and context surrounding an act done without intent to harm [AR].

### Q2. Health test

The second test asks three sub-questions (Q2, "Health test"):

**2a.** "Are there indications of substance abuse?" [V] (Q2, "Health test").
**2b.** "Are there indications of physical ill health?" [V] (Q2, "Health test").
**2c.** "Are there indications of mental ill health?" [V] (Q2, "Health test").

A Yes to 2a routes to Recommendation B (substance-abuse response); a Yes to 2b or 2c routes to Recommendation C (health-issue response); No to all three routes to Q3. The health test is the second filter because the source treats impaired-capacity contributors as conditions to be addressed through occupational-health and substance-abuse-at-work guidance, not as individual culpability [AR].

### Q3. Foresight test

The third test asks three sub-questions (Q3, "Foresight test"):

**3a.** "Are there agreed protocols/accepted practice in place that apply to the action/omission in question?" [V] (Q3, "Foresight test").
**3b.** "Were the protocols/accepted practice workable and in routine use?" [V] (Q3, "Foresight test").
**3c.** "Did the individual knowingly depart from these protocols?" [V] (Q3, "Foresight test").

Yes to all three routes to Q4; No to any of 3a, 3b, or 3c routes to Recommendation D (not appropriate to single out — foresight branch). The foresight test is the load-bearing test for whether the system held up its side of the contract: if there is no agreed protocol (3a), or the protocol is unworkable or not in routine use (3b), or the individual did not knowingly depart from it (3c), then the locus of the problem is upstream of the individual [AR]. The test isolates the case of *knowing departure from a workable, routinely-used, agreed protocol* — the conjunction of all three sub-conditions — as the only path that proceeds further toward potential individual accountability.

### Q4. Substitution test

The fourth test asks three sub-questions (Q4, "Substitution test"):

**4a.** "Are there indications that other individuals from the same peer group, with comparable experience and qualifications, would behave in the same way in similar circumstances?" [V] (Q4, "Substitution test").
**4b.** "Was the individual missed out when relevant training was provided to their peer group?" [V] (Q4, "Substitution test").
**4c.** "Did more senior members of the team fail to provide supervision that normally should be provided?" [V] (Q4, "Substitution test").

Yes to any of 4a, 4b, or 4c routes to Recommendation E (not appropriate to single out — substitution branch); No to all three routes to Q5. The substitution test asks whether a peer in the same circumstances would have done the same thing (4a), whether the individual was structurally disadvantaged by training gaps (4b), or whether ordinary supervision was absent (4c). Any positive finding routes to system-level corrective action rather than to individual management action [AR]. The test's name — substitution — captures the operational question: would substituting another comparable individual into the same situation have produced a different outcome?

### Q5. Mitigating circumstances

The fifth and final test asks a single question (Q5, "Mitigating circumstances"):

**5a.** "Were there any significant mitigating circumstances?" [V] (Q5, "Mitigating circumstances").

Yes routes to Recommendation F (action directed at the individual may not be appropriate); No routes to Recommendation G (management action plus wider investigation). Q5 is the residual: only the case that reaches it has cleared the deliberate-harm filter (Q1), the health filter (Q2), the foresight filter (Q3 — knowing departure from a workable agreed protocol), and the substitution filter (Q4 — no peer-behaviour, training-gap, or supervision-failure exoneration). At that residual point, the final question is whether *significant mitigating circumstances* attach to the case [AR].

## Part III: The seven recommendation end-states

Seven distinct recommendations exit the tree, distributed across the five tests. Three recommendations (D, E, F) route away from management action directed at the individual; two recommendations (A, G) route toward management action directed at the individual; two recommendations (B, C) route toward health-affecting-work pathways that are themselves not punitive but require occupational-health or substance-abuse-at-work response. Every recommendation pairs its operational guidance with a directive about *wider investigation* — patient-safety outcomes are not exhausted by the individual response.

### Recommendation A: deliberate-harm response (exits from Q1 Yes)

> "Follow organisational guidance for appropriate management action. This could involve: contact relevant regulatory bodies, suspension of staff, and referral to police and disciplinary processes. Wider investigation is still needed to understand how and why patients were not protected from the actions of the individual."
>
> [V] (Q1, "Recommendation A")

The deliberate-harm path is the only branch the source pairs with potential police referral and regulatory body contact. Even so, the recommendation closes with "wider investigation is still needed to understand how and why patients were not protected from the actions of the individual" — the system question is not extinguished by the finding of deliberate harm [AR].

### Recommendation B: substance-abuse response (exits from Q2 Yes to 2a)

> "Follow organisational substance abuse at work guidance. Wider investigation is still needed to understand if substance abuse could have been recognised and addressed earlier."
>
> [V] (Q2, "Recommendation B")

The response is procedural (organisational substance-abuse-at-work guidance) plus a backward-looking system question: could the substance abuse have been recognised and addressed earlier? [AR]

### Recommendation C: health-issue response (exits from Q2 Yes to 2b or 2c)

> "Follow organisational guidance for health issues affecting work, which is likely to include occupational health referral. Wider investigation is still needed to understand if health issues could have been recognised and addressed earlier."
>
> [V] (Q2, "Recommendation C")

Same backward-looking system question, this time applied to physical or mental ill health rather than substance abuse [AR].

### Recommendation D: not appropriate to single out — foresight branch (exits from Q3 No to any)

> "Action singling out the individual is unlikely to be appropriate; the patient safety incident investigation should indicate the wider actions needed to improve safety for future patients. These actions may include, but not be limited to, the individual."
>
> [V] (Q3, "Recommendation D")

The phrasing "may include, but not be limited to, the individual" is deliberate: the recommendation neither prescribes nor forecloses individual-directed action; it places the locus of corrective work at the system level, with individual-directed action as one possible component of the wider package [AR].

### Recommendation E: not appropriate to single out — substitution branch (exits from Q4 Yes to any)

> "Action singling out the individual is unlikely to be appropriate; the patient safety incident investigation should indicate the wider actions needed to improve safety for future patients. These actions may include, but not be limited to, the individual."
>
> [V] (Q4, "Recommendation E")

Recommendations D and E are *textually identical*; they differ in their gating test, not their content. The repetition is structural to the poster — the same default response applies whether the exoneration arises from a foresight finding (no workable protocol, no knowing departure) or from a substitution finding (peer behaviour, training gap, supervision absence) [AR].

### Recommendation F: mitigating circumstances apply (exits from Q5 Yes)

> "Action directed at the individual may not be appropriate; follow organisational guidance, which is likely to include senior HR advice on what degree of mitigation applies. The patient safety incident investigation should indicate the wider actions needed to improve safety for future patients."
>
> [V] (Q5, "Recommendation F")

The shift from "is unlikely to be appropriate" (D and E) to "may not be appropriate" (F) is a softer formulation: the case has cleared the prior filters, so the question is open and turns on the degree of mitigation. The recommendation routes the operator to "senior HR advice on what degree of mitigation applies" [V] [AR].

### Recommendation G: management action plus wider investigation (exits from Q5 No)

> "Follow organisational guidance for appropriate management action. This could involve individual training, performance management, competency assessments, changes to role or increased supervision, and may require relevant regulatory bodies to be contacted, staff suspension and disciplinary processes. The patient safety incident investigation should indicate the wider actions needed to improve safety for future patients."
>
> [V] (Q5, "Recommendation G")

The G end-state is the residual after all prior filters have routed elsewhere: deliberate harm absent (Q1 No), health factors absent (Q2 No to all), foresight present (Q3 Yes to all — workable protocol, in routine use, knowingly departed from), substitution-exoneration absent (Q4 No to all — peers would not have behaved the same, training was not missed, supervision was provided), and no significant mitigating circumstances (Q5 No). At that residual, the recommendation lists a graduated set of management responses — "individual training, performance management, competency assessments, changes to role or increased supervision" — before reaching for regulatory bodies, suspension, and disciplinary processes [AR]. As with all other recommendations, the wider investigation is preserved as a parallel obligation, not displaced by the individual response.

## Key statistics (with provenance)

The source carries no quantitative claims — no incidence rates, no outcome statistics, no comparative data on adoption. It is a normative decision aid; the only "data" in the source are the structural counts that arise from its design.

| Metric | Value | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Number of sequential tests in the decision tree | 5 | [V] (Q1-Q5) |
| Number of sub-questions in the tree | 11 (1+3+3+3+1) | [V] (Q1-Q5) |
| Number of distinct recommendation end-states | 7 (A through G, with D and E textually identical) | [V] (Q1-Q5 recommendations) |
| Number of explicit caveats on use | 4 | [V] (Preamble, "Please note") |
| Number of recommendation paths that route to *not* singling out the individual | 4 of 7 (B, C, D, E — and F routes to "may not be appropriate", a softer formulation) | [AP] (Q1-Q5 recommendations) |
| Number of recommendation paths that route toward management action directed at the individual | 2 of 7 (A and G); B and C route to occupational-health pathways; F is conditional | [AP] (Q1-Q5 recommendations) |

## Connections the author makes in the text

- **Professor James Reason.** The poster's footer attributes the guide to "the work of Professor James Reason". Reason is named once, in the source's footer; no specific work or concept of Reason's is cited by name (Footer, "Based on the work of Professor James Reason and the National Patient Safety Agency's Incident Decision Tree") [BT].
- **National Patient Safety Agency's Incident Decision Tree.** The source attributes its structure to the NPSA's Incident Decision Tree. No further detail or citation is provided in the source itself (Footer) [BT].

The poster cites only these two lineages. It does not name any other author, framework, or work.

## Positions the author explicitly frames against

The source does not explicitly frame against named positions or authors. Its argumentative work is structural: the decision tree is built to surface system, training, supervision, and mitigating contributors *before* the individual. The implicit frame is against a *default to individual blame* — the position the source describes as "the tendency to treat wider patient safety issues as individual issues" (Preamble, "Purpose and use"). The source treats this tendency as a pattern to be corrected by structural means (the tree), not as a position attached to a named author or school [AR].

## Citation and source-integrity notes

- **Conversion path.** The source PDF (1.5MB, A3 landscape, single page) was converted with `pymupdf4llm` (default settings). The conversion produced a 156-line markdown rendering that preserved all textual content of the poster including the four "Please note" bullets, the three preamble paragraphs, all five tests with their sub-questions, and all seven recommendation end-states. The text extraction was complete; the only loss was the visual spatial flow of the poster, which is replaced in the converted markdown by a logical nested-heading structure (preamble → five tests in sequence → recommendations grouped under the test that exits to them).

- **Citation style decision.** The single-page poster has no page anchors. Citation style is therefore `(Section, "Subsection")` keyed to the test number (Q1-Q5) or preamble section, with recommendation end-states labelled A-G in the converted markdown to make them individually citable. The original poster does not letter its recommendations; the A-G labels are an ingestion convenience and have no authority in the source. The labels are stable across the deep reference, light reference, and distillations to keep cross-references consistent.

- **Coverage.** Complete. The single-page source has been read in full and every textual element is represented in the deep reference. No section has been omitted; no claim in the deep reference depends on content not present in the converted markdown.

- **Footer attribution.** The source attributes its lineage to "the work of Professor James Reason and the National Patient Safety Agency's Incident Decision Tree". The deep reference reports this attribution as `[BT]` (borrowed-through) — Reason and the NPSA Incident Decision Tree are named by the source, and any further characterisation of either lineage is outside the scope of this deep reference because it is not stated in the source.

Derivative artefacts in this corpus (light reference, distillations, build-profile extracts) inherit only the attribution requirement.
